blockdesc-pipe-bend*: A 90° bend in a pipe run.
blockdesc-pipe-passthroughbend-*: A bent pipe set in masonry.
blockdesc-pipe-outlet*: Connects a pipe run to a machine's port face.
blockdesc-pipe-fluidintake-*: Draws fresh water into the pipe network.
blockdesc-boilercornish-*: Normal single-flue boiler for low steam production.
blockdesc-boilerlancashire-*: Larger twin-flue boiler for high steam production.
blockdesc-enginecornish-*: High-pressure beam engine that's steam intake can be throttled. Drives one attached sub-machine.
blockdesc-enginewatt-*: Low-pressure beam engine — the first step into steam power. Drives one attached sub-machine.
blockdesc-enginefluidpump-*: Engine-driven pump. Transfers water from intake line to pressurised output line.
blockdesc-enginempgenerator-*: Engine-driven crank that turns axles, supplying mechanical power to machines.
blockdesc-steamcondenser-*: Condenses steam from its steam line back into water on its water line.
chimney-info-venting: Venting pipe network: {0:F0} L/s of gas
chimney-info-idle: Capping pipe network (no gas to vent)
boiler-info-needswater: Needs at least {0:F0} L of water to start boiling
engine-throttle-max: The control rods are already wide open (High).
engine-throttle-min: The control rods are already fully closed (Low).
engine-repair-wrench: Hold a wrench to repair the engine.
structure-incomplete-count: Structure is not complete. {0} blocks are missing or incorrect.
game:placefailure-ppex-fluidintake-nowat: The fluid intake must be placed directly on top of a water block.
blockhelp-boiler-lid: Hold to open or close the lid
handbook-steampower-text:
Pipes and Power Expanded adds pipe networks and modular steam machinery. This way you can produce mechanical power just from boiling water, but it is expensive in terms of materials and may lead to catastrophic events! So be sure you are well established and has achieved iron age before you attempt to build your first boiler.
The general ides is for you to route everything as: water supply == boiler == engine == sub-machine . So you need to build a working loop, that will feed itself, while burning coal and producing the power you want.
This article covers only the basic pipes that connect everything, see Steam Power: Boilers, Steam Power: Engines and Steam Power: Fittings for the information about the machines.

How pipe networks work
Connected pipes form one network with a shared pool. A network carries one medium at a time — a gas (air, blast, exhaust, steam) or water, never both. Each pipe adds 30 L of capacity, which get's added together for total network capacity. Pressure is simply volume over capacity, shown in atm (atmosphere). The whole network also shares one temperature.

Materials matter. An iron pipe bursts above 5 atm, a steel pipe is stronger and can take pressure up to 10 atm, and the weakest pipe limits the whole run. An over-pressured run holds for some time, then pipes can start to break.

Open ends leak. Any unconnected pipe connector bleeds the network (≈8 L/s of gas, 10 L/s of water), so a leaking run can never build pressure (leaking pipe cannot accumulate pressure past 1atm). Cap runs against machine ports, valves or outlets. Standing water in pipes and boilers also slowly evaporates (≈50 L per day).

Building runs. Straights, bends, T- and X-junctions snap their orientation to neighbouring pipes when placed; rotate a piece with a wrench if it guessed wrong. Pipes connect to machines through their port faces (boiler outlets, engine inlets, pump and intake flanges) — butt the pipe straight against the port.
handbook-boilers-text:
Boilers allow to burn coal near a water vessel and push steam into a pipe network. Boiling 1 L of water yields 16 L of steam, at 160-220 °C. This steam builds up inside the boiler and it's connected steam network.

Construction. Place the boiler block, then rightmouse it repeatedly with the required materials in your hotbar to raise it through its construction stages. The boiler also needs a masonry firebox built around its base — ctrl + shift + rightmouse shows the outline, and the chat reports what is missing.

Cornish Boiler is a basic single flue boiler that can be used for supplying the machines with low pressure steam. It is recommended for you to start building these before you go for the next tier. Materials required for it's construction:
- 24 x Metal Plate (iron or steel)
- 18 x Metal Nails & Strips (iron or steel)
- 8 x Metal Rod (iron or steel)
- 48 x Fire Brick
Firebox masonry: 33 x Fire Brick Blocks, 1 x Coke Oven Door, 2 x Fire Brick Pipe Passthrough, 1 x Fire Brick Passthrough Bend (up), 1 x Fire Brick Pipe Outlet (up), one empty slot for the coal pile.
This boiler outputs up to 32 L/s of steam and chokes at 5 atm. Vessel holds 800 L while requiring 150 L of water to actually boil.

Lancashire Boiler is bigger and more advanced boiler that can produce higher pressure steam at bigger volumes. This one is required to operate the machinery that requires higher pressures, but be ready for the steel amounts needed to build one of these:
- 36 x Steel Plate
- 24 x Metal Nails & Strips (iron or steel)
- 12 x Metal Rod (iron or steel)
- 64 x Fire Brick
Firebox masonry: 39 x Fire Brick Blocks plus the same door, passthroughs and outlet as the Cornish.
This boiler can output up to 48 L/s of steam and can reach up to 12 atm of pressure. It's vessel holds 1200 L while requiring at least 200 L of water to begin boiling.

Operating a boiler. You need to kickstart the system manually with buckets, after which the running boiler can power the engine for the fluid pump so it will start refilling automatically. The key is to reach steam pressure required for the engine to start operating before the manually filled water runs out. If this operation goes smoothly, the pumping engine will be able to supply your boilers so they will run while the coal is burning.
1. Hold rightmouse on the lid to open it, pour in water (buckets, or pipe pressurised water into the intake from a fluid pump), and close the lid.
2. Lay a coal pile in the firebox slot behind the door and ignite it.
3. After about three minutes of heating the boiler starts boiling and pushes steam out its top outlet. This is what needs to go to your engines!
4. The firebox exhaust must breathe: vent its outlet through a chimney or pipe. If exhaust back-pressure rises above ~0.8 atm the fire goes out.

The boilers are dangerous! A boiler that keeps firing with nowhere to push steam and sits at its choke pressure can explode! An open lid vents steam harmlessly (≈100 L/s), this is your emergency release. An unpiped steam outlet just leaks, but it is not enough to prevent the boiler from overpressuring. Feeding pressurised water into a boiling vessel flashes extra steam and spikes the pressure! You need to throttle pumped feed water with a valve or gate it.
Warning! The Cornish boiler's 5 atm ceiling is above the Watt engine's 4 atm breaking point. Put a pressure valve between boiler and engine and create an outflow pipe to keep the pressure under control.
handbook-engines-text:
Engines take steam from a pipe network and drive one attached sub-machine. Place the engine first (it raises through right-click construction stages, like boilers), then place its sub-machine in the cell just under the pumping rod. Submachine snaps into the right facing on its own.

Watt Engine is an engine that operates on large amounts of low pressure steam. It is relatively cheap and can be used to stand up your first steam power setups. One Cornish boiler can safely power one of these engines, and it:
- Runs at 2-4 atm inlet steam; above 4 atm it wears down fast and can break!
- Draws 30 L/s of steam, delivers 0.3 power (enough to power 2 helve hammers without the speed dropping), and dribbles ~1 L/s of hot condensate out its drain. You can pipe it away to reuse the water or it spills on the floor.
- Construction stages consume: 4 x Metal Plate, 24 x Metal Rod, 12 x Metal Nails & Strips, all iron or steel and 36 fire bricks.

Cornish Engine is more advanced engine that works on higher pressured steam. It consumes less steam volume to produce the same power as Watt engine, and since it can regulate the amount of steam entering the cylinder - you can throttle this engine:
- Runs at 6-8 atm. Its steam control rod sets the working rate: Low / Normal / High that will draw 8 / 16 / 32 L/s of steam respectively and deliver 0.2 / 0.4 / 0.8 units of power. So overclocked Cornish engine can easily power 6 helve hammers, and throttling also affects the amount of water is pumped using the fluid pump.
- Construction stages consume the same as watt engine, the difference in cost is in the main block.

Sub-machines are the blocks that consume engine's power to do actual work (one per engine, placed in the engine's drive cell). The steam engine is only the power provider, so depending on different sub-machines it can do different work:
- MP Generator — turns engine's power into usable mechanical power. It is a constant-power source for the axles: within the engine's rated load it holds normal speed. If you pile on more machines and the whole network slows past roughly double the rated load it overstresses and stops. A Watt engine comfortably runs a couple of helve hammers, a Cornish engine on High can probably run a whole workshop.
- Fluid Pump — transfers water from its intake line into its output line, about ~5 L/s at full engine power. Its output is pressurised to ~75% of the engine's inlet steam pressure — enough to push water up into a boiler. Pumps double the amount of water if powered by overclocked Cornish engine.
- Air Blower (from Steelmaking Expanded) — pressurises air into Blast for the blast furnace and Bessemer converter. It also cannot pressurize the air more than ~75% of the engine's inlet steam pressure, so manage your steam levels with pressure valves to achieve required pressure. This sub-machine produces ~16 L/s of air if powered by normal running engine and double that when powered by pressured up Cornish.

Breakage and repair. An engine run above its pressure band for too long breaks: it's cylinder explodes and piston is damaged. Click on it with rightmouse while holding a wrench to see the repair bill, then with the materials in your hotbar to fix it. Mind the Cornish-boiler-into-Watt-engine trap: that boiler can push 5 atm, the Watt breaks at 4 — regulate with a pressure valve.
handbook-fittings-text:
The smaller blocks that can shape a pipe network:

Valve — a hand-operated shut-off in the line. rightmouse toggles it: open, the line flows through; closed, the network is severed at the valve and the two sides become independent. Sits flush against machine ports.

Pressure Valve — a directional overflow. It holds back its input side until the pressure rises above its gate pressure, then lets the excess through to its output side; wrench it to flip the direction, rightmouse / ctrl + rightmouse to raise or lower the gate. With nothing piped on the output it vents the excess like a leak. The essential regulator between a strong boiler and a weak engine. The input side is marked with copper textured ring.

Steam Condenser — used to convert unused steam back into water to safely recycle it. The condenser pulls up to 30 L/s of steam from its steam port and adds the condensed water (1 L per 16 L of steam) to its separate water line, which passes through it at up to 50 L/s. Use it to reclaim boiler feed water and to relieve steam pressure.

Fluid Intake — draws fresh water from open water into the network. It must sit directly on a full cube of water at least 3 blocks deep, and refuses to work within 6 blocks of another intake. Feed it into a fluid pump to lift the water onward.

Passthroughs & Outlets — masonry blocks with a pipe cast through them, so structure walls (boiler fireboxes, cowper stoves, furnaces) can be built across a pipe run. An outlet ends a run against a machine port face; topped with a vanilla chimney it instead vents the network's gas to the sky at ~16 L/s — the standard way to exhaust a firebox.
handbook-steampowerguide-text:
To build a minimal working steam line you need:

1. Water: place a Fluid Intake on a pond (full 3x3-deep water below it).
2. Boiler: build a Cornish Boiler with its firebox; fill it with water (buckets work for the first run) and light a coal pile behind its door. Vent the firebox outlet with a chimney.
3. Engine: build a Watt Engine nearby and pipe the boiler's steam outlet to the engine inlet through a Pressure Valve gated at ~2–3 atm (the Cornish boiler can out-pressure a Watt engine).
4. Sub-machine: drop a Fluid Pump or MP Generator into the engine's drive cell. Pump: pipe the fluid intake to its intake side and its output up into the boiler — the water loop closes itself. Generator: hook up axles and run your helve hammers and quern.
5. Pipe the engine's condensate drain back toward the boiler or a drain — and you have a self-feeding steam plant.

From here you can add more boilers and engines to produce mechanical power or transition to a Lancashire Boiler feeding a Cornish Engine. Best use for the steam power right now it to power the machines required for the blast furnace (air blowers) and the Bessemer converter from Steelmaking Expanded, so you can produce enough iron and steel to satisfy your Industrial Revolution needs.
